1: <header>Life cycle analysis of a biogas-centred integrated dairy farm-greenhouse system in British Columbia </header>
Siduo Zhanga, Xiaotao Tony Bia,* , Roland Clift

a Chemical and Biological Engineering Department, University of British Columbia, 2360 East Mall, Vancouver, BC V6T 1Z3, Canada b Centre for Environmental Strategy, University of Surrey, Guildford, Surrey GU2 7XH, United Kingdom
2: This study investigates the potential integration of a dairy farm and a greenhouse into an eco-industrial system to promote waste-to-energy and waste-to-material exchanges. Natural gas consumption is substituted by renewable biogas generated from anaerobic digestion (AD) of the dairy manure; CO2 for plant enrichment in greenhouses is supplied by biogas combustion and the digestate (digestion residue) from digesters is used as animal bedding, plant growing media and liquid fertilizers.

A life cycle analysis (LCA) was conducted to quantify the environmental impacts of the eco-industrial system in comparison to the conventional agriculture practices. The results show that the integrated system reduces non- renewable energy consumption, climate change impact, acidification, respiratory effects from organic emissions, and human toxicity by more than 40%. If the digestate surplus is treated as a waste, the integrated system shows an increase in eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions while all the analyzed impacts are reduced if the digestate can be used for substituting chemical fertilizers.

Keywords: Industrial Ecology; Renewable energy; Energy integration; Climate change; Life cycle analysis; Environ- mental impact assessment
3: Livestock farms in British Columbia (BC) generate 2.7 million tonnes of manure each year which accounts for 82% of total organic waste in the province (Electrigaz Technologies Inc., 2007). Of this total, 65% comes from the dairy industry (BC Ministry of Agriculture, 2011). Nearly 200 compounds are emit- ted from livestock manure management (O'Neil and Phillips, 1992). The contamination of ground water and surface water is one of the major hazards from manure land application. Therefore, the conventional practice of livestock manure man- agement creates an environmental and public health problem.
Anaerobic digestion (AD) degrades organic matter into biogas (typical composition ~60% CH4 and ~40% CO2). The

material remaining after digestion, known as digestate, can be used as fertilizer or soil improver. AD for manure treat- ment is advantageous over conventional practices in terms of environmental protection. AD requires no storage of the raw manure and eliminates odours and volatile organic matter. AD is also able to kill many weed seeds and pathogens as a result of the warm digestion environment; thus using digestate can avoid the usage of herbicides and the health concerns over pathogen contamination are largely relieved (AFBI, 2012). After digestion, the pollutants in the raw biogas are removed by gas upgrading. AD also retains the nutrients in manure: organic N is degraded into ammonium during digestion and over 20% more of the total nitrogen becomes available for plants com- pared to raw manure (DEFRA, 2010; Sommer et al., 2001).

Another major industrial scale agricultural activity in BC is the greenhouse industry, which relies heavily on natural gas for heating and CO2 enrichment. The natural gas consumed for heating the greenhouses represents about 36% of the total consumption of 2500T by BC's agriculture sector (Khakbazan, 2000). As a fossil fuel, extraction and processing of natural gas involve significant environmental impacts. Burning natural gas emits fossil CO2, a major greenhouse gas (GHG), although part of the CO2 can be utilized in the greenhouse for stimulat- ing plant growth In the summer, heating demand is low while CO2 demand is high as the plants grow robustly; thus signifi- cant amounts of natural gas have to be combusted to provide CO2. Therefore, high natural gas consumption is another prob- lem for BC agriculture.

Industrial Ecology (IE) introduces natural ecosystem princi- ples to industrial systems. When waste from some processes is potentially useful as raw material for other processes, they can be connected just like a food chain in nature. Such an inte- grated industrial system is called an eco-industrial system. With the problems and opportunities identified for BC's agri- culture sector above, this study investigates an eco-industrial system integrating a dairy farm and a greenhouse. In this integrated system, manure is anaerobically digested to pro- duce biogas that substitutes natural gas use. Digestate is separated with the liquid phase used as fertilizer and the solid phase as COW bedding material and greenhouse grow- ing medium. The objective of this study is to explore and quantify the environmental performance of the proposed eco- industrial system. The expected benefits include reduction of non-renewable energy consumption and emissions of air pol- lutants and greenhouse gases, and the improvement of local air quality. A life cycle analysis (LCA) has been conducted to quantify the environmental benefits associated with the eco- industrial system.

7: LCA is a systematic tool for identifying and evaluating the environmental aspects of products and services from extrac- tion of resource inputs to the eventual disposal of the product or its waste ("cradle to grave") (ISO 14044, 2006). LCA pro- vides a complete picture and comprehensive information on the environmental impacts of a product or service, avoid- ing the misleading conclusions that can arise from more limited analysis. To address the important concerns and to reflect the significant impacts from the system analyzed here, non-renewable energy consumption, climate change, aquatic acidification, aquatic eutrophication, respiratory effects and human toxicity are selected as impact indicators for analysis.

Primary non-renewable energy consumption is calculated following the Cumulative Energy Demand (CED) method ver- sion 1.04. More than 99.5% of the non-renewable energy consumption in this study is fossil energy; therefore the total non-renewable energy consumption is analyzed without sub- dividing into specific types of non-renewable energy. Climate change impact is characterized using IPCC's 2007 global warm- ing potential (GWP) factors with a timeframe of 100 years (Solomon et al., 2007). This method only includes the direct GWP of air emissions. CO2 is universally used as the bench- mark compound and the climate change impact is expressed as mass of CO2-equivalent. Aquatic acidification and aquatic

eutrophication are analyzed using the method CML 2001 version 2.05. Impacts on human health are analyzed using IMPACT 2002+ version 2.10. These methods were developed in Europe with the background models representing European conditions. Since the alternative methods are as yet limited, this method is also widely used for regions other than Europe. For acidification and eutrophication, the selected benchmark substances are SO2 and PO4, respectively (Huijbregts, 1999). For human health impact, dose-response relations derived from epidemiological studies are used to determine the tox- icity potential of each hazardous substance (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2000). PM2.5, which is small enough to invade the smallest human airways, is selected as the benchmark sub- stance for inorganic respiratory pollutants, while C2H4 is taken as the benchmark substance for organic respiratory pollut- ants (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2000). For both carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic impacts, the benchmark substance is C2H3Cl, a declared human carcinogen with well-defined fate data with air inhalation as the main exposure pathway (Jolliet et al., 2003).
In this study, the collected environmental data were first organized in Microsoft Excel and the life cycle systems were built up and modelled using licensed LCA software, SimaPro version 7.3.3.

11: To assess the impact reduction contributions in the entire province, the results are normalized by BC or national inven- tories depending on data availability. In the studied system, a dairy farm with 500 COWS is selected to match the energy demand of a 5300 greenhouse. There were 105,000 COWS in BC in 2006 (BC Ministry of Agriculture, 2007). If all the cor- responding dairy manure is used for biogas production, it can supply heat and CO2 demands to roughly 1.1 million m² green- houses, out of the total of 5.3 million m² (Foster et al., 2011) in BC. Therefore, the total potential change for applying eco- industrial integration in BC's dairy sector is 210 (105,000/500) times the results reported here; this point is discussed further in Section 4. Specifically, the year-round total impact reduction potential for the one of the eco-industrial scenarios (moderate scenario - see below) is used in the normalization analysis. Monte Carlo stochastic analysis is used to evaluate the sig- nificance of differences between the base scenario and the eco-industrial system by estimating the probability that the impact of one scenario is higher/lower than that of another (Goedkoop et al., 2010). Results are based on 100 Monte Carlo calculations using the Life Cycle Inventories (LCIs) with esti- mated uncertainties. Wherever the uncertainty is reported in the data source, it is used directly; where no uncertainty data are reported, a 20% uncertainty is applied.
12: The functional unit of this LCA study is selected as the "dis- posal of 1100 tonnes of dairy manure", corresponding to the amount of manure generated by 500 COWS in one month (Electrigaz Technologies Inc., 2007). The size of the green- house is determined to be 5330 m² by matching the biogas generated from AD with the total gas demand in the summer season. The gas demand in the summer is primarily for CO2

14: The conventional system is the base scenario, defined as Sce- nario a. Two scenarios are considered for the eco-industrial system. Scenario b is called the "optimistic scenario" with the surplus digestate after substituting the base demand counted as potentially useful to replace materials elsewhere to avoid production of those materials. However, the surplus digestate may not be useful if local users cannot be found. Scenario C, called the "moderate scenario", describes the possibility that the surplus digestate has to be disposed as a waste.
15: Fig. 1 shows the system boundaries of the base scenario (left) and the integrated eco-industrial system (right) in this study. As the LCA is intended to assess the advantages of the pro- posed eco-industrial system over the conventional system, the system boundaries for the conventional operation and the integrated system are kept strictly consistent and comparable, although the components within the two systems may differ.
Tables 1 and 2 present the basic data of the system and the data at different seasons over a time interval of one month, respectively. The data sources are presented in the comple- mentary material.
16: This section explains the development of the system inven- tory with the specific statistical data, data sources, and emission factors presented in the complementary material.
17: Data for natural gas production and transmission are differen- tiated for industrial use and for secondary energy (electricity) production. Diesel is consumed in heavy duty vehicles (HDV) for transport and tractors for manure and liquid digestate spreading. The data for highway diesel are used for both HDVs and tractors as the consumption associated with tractors is quite small compared to highway vehicles. BC's electricity mix consists of hydro, natural gas, biomass and fuel oil in the proportions of 93.35%, 5.1%, 1.45% and 0.1%, respectively (Environment Canada, 2011). For all sources of electricity, the datasets cover the whole system from upstream extraction through transmission to end use.

Data for cow bedding sawdust cover logging to sawmilling as well as transport using HDVs. The growing medium for the greenhouses in the base scenario is modelled as commer- cial rockwool products. In the eco-industrial system, equal volume of dried solid digestate is assumed to substitute the sawdust and rockwool consumed in the base system while the surplus digestate is modelled as saving the production of equivalent sawdust in the optimistic scenario. The liquid digestate is treated as an organic N fertilizer since digestate has a higher ammonium content. P and K contents usually do not change during digestion (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007). In the optimistic eco-industrial scenario, urea ammonium nitrate is assumed to be substituted by recovered surplus N from liquid digestate.

19: A diesel-fuelled lorry with 20-28 tonnes load capacity is modelled for transporting sawdust, rockwool, and chemical fertilizers over an average distance of km, a reasonable dis- tance within the agriculture area in Lower Mainland of BC (Booy et al., 2011). The return trip is always assumed to be empty. Storage of raw manure is modelled as in a tank on the dairy farm with electricity consumed for pumping (Jungbluth et al., 2007). Stored manure is applied to crop land near the dairy farm using vacuum tank tractor (Nemecek and Kagi, 2007). Composting of the solid digestate surplus in the mod- erate scenario is modelled as windrow composting in which the organic matter is piled in long rows in the open air. The data include the whole process from piling feedstock to end use of compost products but stage-wise breakdowns are not available.

The anaerobic digester is modelled as a CSTR with the data sampled from 20 biogas plants in Switzerland (Jungbluth et al., 2007). Raw manure is assumed to be digested directly without any storage. The dry matter content is assumed to be con- trolled at 12% which falls within the recommended 10-15% wet digestion range. The temperature inside the digester is modelled as stable at 35 °C, corresponding to mesophilic diges- tion (Cant, 2006). Electricity is modelled as from the local grid, and heat is generated from biogas fuelled boilers. The bio- gas yield is assumed to be stable, containing 60% CH4, 37.9% CO2, 2% H2O, and 0.1% H2S, which is a typical composition with trace impurities neglected (BC Ministry of Environment,

2010). Raw biogas from the digester is piped into two adsor- bent columns at elevated pressures, where H2O and H2S are completely removed by limestone and iron milling filings, respectively (Torenvliet, 2011). CO2 is not removed as the bio- gas is used for combustion rather than for engines or grid distribution. No energy consumption is accounted for the flow of biogas through those pipes. The production of the two sor- bent materials is included. Raw digestate is separated into solid and liquid phases by a press-screw separator. Solid diges- tate is assumed to be dried by natural drying. No emission data for digestate separation and drying are available; therefore emission rates are estimated by assuming that 20% of the total land application emissions occur during digestate separation and drying. In addition, the separation process is modelled as taking place in an enclosed space, so that the emission can be further reduced to 20% as suggested by Stucki et al. (2011). Spreading of liquid digestate is modelled as using the same vacuum tank tractor as for manure application. Heat for the farms is supplied by a modulated boiler. The energy efficiency of the boiler is 96% and 90% for burning natural gas and biogas, respectively (Börjesson and Berglund, 2006; Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories, 2010).

These emissions are com- mon species and major contributors to climate change (CO2 fossil, CH4, N2O), eutrophication and acidification (NOx, SOx, NH2, H2S), and human health (NH3, H2S, NMVOC, and PMs), respectively. Overall, the LCI results show that under the optimistic cir- cumstances, PMs, NMVOCs, SOx emissions have significant reductions (>100%) in all seasons. for the different seasons. Table 4 summarizes the absolute emission reductions for the eco-industrial system compared to the base scenario. It is noted that not all emission species are reduced in the moderate scenarios of the integrated eco- industrial system.

Overall, the LCI results show that under the optimistic cir- cumstances, PMs, NMVOCs, SOx emissions have significant reductions (>100%) in all seasons. GHG emissions such as CO2, CH4, and N2O are also all reduced substantially (by more than 80% of the year-round average). In the moderate sce- nario where surplus digestate is composted instead of being used to replace resources, the reductions are less significant; some emissions such as NOx and PMs even show values much higher than the base scenario, for reasons explained below. Any seasonal variations result from variations in use of sup- plementary natural gas.
28: About 90% of the fossil CO2 emissions in the base scenario come from natural gas combustion, with natural gas upstream processing as the second significant source of greenhouse gases. In the eco-industrial system, composting of solid diges- tate surplus appears to be the biggest contributor in the moderate scenario; it is inferred that diesel and electricity uses are responsible for most fossil CO2 emissions. The operation of the biogas system accounts for the rest of the fossil CO2 emis- sions in the eco-industrial system, arising from the energy use. In the optimistic scenario, significant fossil CO2 reductions are observed from using surplus digestate to replace sawdust and urea ammonium nitrate, which lead to negative life cycle emissions.
29: Production and distribution of natural gas account for about 90% of the fossil CH4 emissions in the base scenario. In the eco- industrial system, the AD process is the biggest contributor and the overall results follow a similar pattern to the fossil CO2 emissions, with these emissions arising from electricity and diesel use. Also in the optimistic scenario, significant reduc- tions can be realized by substituting sawdust and nitrogen fertilizers with surplus digestate.
30: Almost all the biogenic CH4 emissions arise from manure storage in the base scenario, because of the anaerobic envi- ronment in the storage tank. CH4 emission factors for land spreading of manure are reported to be one order of magni- tude lower than for manure storage (Dong et al., 2006; Amon et al., 2006). In the eco-industrial system, the CH4 emissions are mainly from AD and biogas upgrading, in the form of bio- gas leakages. Only trace amounts of emissions are reported to be associated with production of rockwool and sawdust; therefore, net savings in the optimistic scenario are negligible.
31: N2O, another GHG, is emitted intensively from manure stor- age and spreading in the base scenario. N2O generation is more favoured in aerobic conditions, so that manure spread- ing creates higher emissions. Digestate provides less energy for the N2O-forming microorganisms because the remaining organic matter decomposes more slowly; this leads to lower N2O emissions from digestate application compared to raw manure spreading (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007). N2O emis- sions are significant in urea ammonium nitrate production

due to the intermediate product nitric acid (Wood and Cowie, 2004); thus greater reductions in N2O emissions are achieved from the substitution of nitrogen fertilizers by liquid digestate surplus in the optimistic scenario.
32: NOx emissions in the base scenario arise mostly from nat- ural gas upstream processing, natural gas combustion, and manure spreading, as a result of combustion of fuels directly or indirectly. In the eco-industrial systems, NOx emissions are mainly from biogas combustion, solid digestate composting, and liquid digestate spreading, of which the biogas combus- tion is the dominant source. NOx emission factor for biogas combustion is higher than that for natural gas combustion. Therefore, the moderate eco-industrial scenario has a higher NOx emission than the base scenario (year-round average +36%). Even in the optimistic scenario where some emissions are reduced from substituted sawdust and nitrogen fertilizer production, the net reduction in NOx emissions is very limited.
33: Most of the SOx emissions in the base scenario are from natural gas upstream processing with fuel production and natural gas sweetening as the main contributors (Delucchi and Levelton, 2012). The second biggest source is rockwool production. Other SOx emissions in this system arise from combustion of fossil fuels. These include natural gas com- bustion, manure spreading, and sawdust production in the base scenario and surplus digestate composting, AD, and bio- gas upgrading in the eco-industrial system. In the optimistic scenario, significant SOx emissions are avoided from substitu- tion of nitrogen fertilizer and sawdust and a negative life cycle emission is observed.
34: NH3 emissions in the base scenario arise predominantly from manure storage and spreading. In the eco-industrial system, liquid digestate spreading is the most significant source of emissions. Although the emission factor for digestate is higher than that for raw manure due to the presence of more ammo- nium in the digestate, the total NH3 emission from digestate is lower than from manure since less tonnage of diges- tate is required when there is more plant-available nitrogen (ammonium). Composting of surplus digestate in the mod- erate scenario of the eco-industrial system contributes the second largest NH3 emission from decomposition of organic nitrogen compounds. NH3 emissions from sawdust and urea ammonium nitrate production are very small, so that there is barely any reduction of NH3 emissions in the optimistic scenario over the moderate scenario.
35: Similar to NH3 emissions, almost all H2S emissions in the base scenario come from manure storage and manure spreading. Emissions from the storage stage are higher than from land spreading since H2S is produced under anaerobic conditions (Atia et al., 1999). In the eco-industrial system, H2S emission stems from the leakage of biogas since H2S is one of the main impurities of raw biogas. The total amount is much lower (almost 100% reduction from base scenario) as biogas is prop- erly upgraded. Emission reduction from using the digestate in the optimistic scenario is limited.
36: Most NMVOCs emissions in the base scenario come from nat- ural gas extraction, processing, and combustion. The main NMVOC species are pentane, butane, and benzene (Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories, 2010). Sawdust production (Pa, 2010) and rockwool production (Nielsen et al., 2003) also generate NMVOC emissions resulting from fuel refining and use. In the eco-industrial system, all NMVOCs are reported from indirect processes of energy and material production and use. Biogas upgrading and liquid digestate spreading account for most of the NMVOCs. Limestone milling is the major con- tributor in biogas upgrading stage and diesel production and usage are the main emission sources in digestate spread- ing. NMVOCs from AD and biogas combustion are due to the electricity use; no data are available for possible direct emissions. Urea ammonium nitrate production and sawdust production generate significant amount of NMVOC emissions mainly from production of ammonia and nitric acid (Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories, 2010) and consumption of energy. In the optimistic scenario, total NMVOC emissions from the biogas system are fairly low compared to the sav- ings from substituted sawdust and nitrogen fertilizers SO that the net emission is far below zero.
37: Emissions of PMs with different diameters show different attributional patterns in the inventories. It is worth noting that some data sources do not have detailed information for specific sizes of the PMs; therefore their emissions are cat- egorized with the PMs with a diameter greater than 10 um. In the base scenario, natural gas combustion emits one order of magnitude more PM2.5 than PMs with a diameter 10m. Urea ammonium nitrate production produces similar amounts of PM2.5, PM10, and other PM. Rockwool production also causes significant PM10 emissions, originating from fossil fuel processing (Nielsen et al., 2003). Sawdust production gen- erates some PM2.5 emissions from steam generation (Pa, 2010). Manure storage contributes to some PM10 emissions indirectly from electricity generation. For other PM, sawdust production is the biggest contributor, primarily from the process of wood sawing (Pa, 2010). Rockwool and natural gas production are the second significant contributors to other PM emissions. Manure spreading also creates PM emissions as the process certainly gives rise to dust from the land. In the eco-industrial system, AD generates PM emissions indirectly from electricity production with no data available for possible direct emis- sions. The biogas upgrading stage contributes significantly to PM emissions due to manufacturing and disposal of milled limestone. Combustion of biogas has unspecified PMs from direct emissions and from upstream electricity production. In the moderate scenario, the PM emissions from biogas upgrad- ing and combustion are tremendous, leading to a total value several times higher than the base scenario. In the optimistic scenario, PM emission savings from substituted nitrogen fer- tilizer and sawdust are shown to be significant; especially for PM10 and PM2.5 emissions, the life cycle emissions are negative in the optimistic scenario.
38: The absolute impact assessment results with stage-wise data are presented in Fig. 2. All numerical values are provided in the complementary material.
39: More than 99% of the non-renewable energy consumption in this system is from natural gas. For the base scenario, summer consumption of non-renewable energy is the highest due to CO2 enrichment demand. Natural gas requirements in spring and fall are also partly attributed to CO2 enrichment demands, which are about 400 GJ/month lower than in the summer (Luczynski and Portree, 2005). Winter shows the sec- ond highest non-renewable energy consumption as a result of high heating demand. By conducting the eco-industrial integration, the overall non-renewable energy consumption is dramatically reduced by about 12,600 GJ/year (~74% reduc- tion over the base scenario) in the moderate scenario. The most significant drop occurs in the summer, when all of the 1622 GJ/month natural gas is avoided by the use of the 37,000m (~710 GJ)/month biogas generated from the digester. CO2 for greenhouse CO2 enrichment can be supplied entirely from the biogas because of its high CO2 content (~40%). Non-renewable energy consumption associated with sawdust production, rockwool production and manure spreading are also avoided in the eco-industrial scenarios. AD and diges- tate spreading consume non-renewable energy as well but the amount is insignificant by comparison. In the optimistic sce- nario, non-renewable energy reductions are further achieved from the substitution of sawdust and nitrogen fertilizer at an amount of about 0 GJ/month.
40: Natural gas combustion is a significant source of fossil CO2 emissions and natural gas upstream stages emit fossil CO2 and CH4 emissions. Thus, the climate change impact is largely correlated with natural gas consumption. In the base scenario, manure storage and spreading also contribute 65 tonne CO2- equiv/month. In the eco-industrial system, manure storage and spreading and part of the natural gas consumption are avoided. The AD reactor contributes a small impact due to the electricity use as well as CH4 leakage. Liquid digestate spread- ing also has some limited impact mainly as a result of N2O emissions. Overall, the abatement of climate change impact is around 1360 tonne CO2-equiv/year (81% reduction over the base scenario) in the moderate scenario of the integrated sys- tem. In the optimistic scenario, reduction in climate change impact from reused surplus digestate contributes a further 8 tonne CO2-equiv/month.
41: In the base scenario, manure spreading is the primary source of acidification impact. The emissions that lead to acidifica- tion are on-site and direct; these include H2S, NH3, SO2, and NOx emissions. The second largest contributor to acidifica- tion impact is manure storage due to H2S and NH3 emissions. The reduction in the moderate scenario is about 724 kg SO2- equiv/month (40% lower than the base scenario). Digestate application generates lower emissions of H2S, NH3, SO2, and NOx than manure spreading with an impact reduction of 427 kg SO2-equiv/month. The biogas combustion stage gen- erates NOx and NH3 emissions at 30 kg SO2-equiv/month. For the moderate scenario, composting of the surplus digestate also contributes a significant acidification impact (268 kg SO2- equiv/month), mainly due to the relatively high NOx and SO2 emissions. The reduction in acidification impact from sub- stituted nitrogen fertilizer and sawdust is very limited, SO that the optimistic scenario is not significantly better than the moderate scenario. In total, the decrease in acidification

0: impact by setting up an eco-industrial system can be about 2170kg SO2-equiv/year.

storage generates 36 kg PO4-equiv/month and manure spread- ing generates 182kg PO4-equiv/month. The overall eutro- phication impact of digestate application is still significant but less than that caused by raw manure. 6: In the base scenario, upstream processing of natural gas generates the highest respiratory effects from organic pol- lutants emissions. Natural gas combustion is the second largest source of respiratory effects with the on-site emis- sions mainly as pentane, butane, and benzene. Sawdust production and rockwool production contribute about 0.35 C2H4-equiv/month each, due to NMVOC emissions. In the eco-industrial system, the impact resulting from natural gas production and consumption is still noticeable in spring, fall, and winter seasons but is much lower than the base sce- nario as less natural gas is required. Contributions from other stages including composting of surplus digestate are insignif- icant. Therefore, the difference between the optimistic and moderate scenarios comes from the reuse of surplus diges- tate. Production of sawdust and urea ammonium nitrate emits fossil CH4 and NMVOCs (Section 4.2). About 1.2kg C2H4- equiv/month is reduced in the optimistic scenario by the use of surplus digestate, which results in a net impact of -0.34kg C2H4-equiv/month. As noted above for respiratory effects from inorganic emissions, these impacts can be geographically dis- persed since the material production processes are not limited to one area. The respiratory effects can be further analyzed based on specific receptors.
7: Human toxicity impact is associated with all stages: manure management, natural gas, and materials production. In the base scenario, manure storage and spreading con- tribute equally to non-carcinogenic toxicity (~80 kg C2H3Cl- equiv/month) from emissions of H2S and NH3. Natural gas combustion generates both non-carcinogenic and carcino- genic toxicity, of which the carcinogenic toxicity is mainly due to the organic emissions (NMVOCs). Rockwool produc- tion has an impact of kg C2H3Cl-equiv/month, which includes both non-carcinogenic and carcinogenic toxicity mainly due to the use of coal (Nielsen et al., 2003). In the eco-industrial system, natural gas combustion remains part of the sources of toxicity impact in spring, fall, and

winter but liquid digestate spreading is the most signifi- cant contributor (22kg C2H3Cl-equiv/month). Similar to the manure spreading, spreading digestate contributes to non- carcinogens from inorganic species released on-site. Biogas upgrading generates toxicity impacts from both on-site emis- sions and adsorbent production. In the moderate scenario, composting of the surplus digestate creates non-carcinogenic toxicity. In the optimistic scenario, significant reduction is achieved from using digestate to substitute nitrogen fertilizer. Toxicity associated with the production of urea ammonium nitrate is both non-carcinogenic and carcinogenic, arising mostly from ammonia and nitric acid production. The total toxicity impact of the optimistic scenario is on average 7kg C2H3Cl-equiv/month while the year-round reductions in the moderate and optimistic scenarios compared to the base scenario are 2190 kg C2H3Cl-equiv/year and 2870kg C2H3Cl- equiv/year, respectively.

9: The direct reduction of natural gas consumption in the eco- industrial system is about 10 TJ/year, and the entire dairy industry in BC could potentially save about TJ/year. Total consumption of natural gas in the agriculture sector of BC in 2010 was 5700TJ (Nyboer and Kniewasser, 2012). Thus the potential savings are up to 37% of the total consumption in the BC agriculture sector. For the total non-renewable primary energy consumption, the reduction is about TJ/year for the specific eco-industrial system assessed; this translates into potential savings of 2700 TJ/year for the dairy industry in BC. Compared to the estimated total primary energy input to the BC economy of about 22,500T in 2010, the potential reduction of non-renewable energy consumption in BC's agriculture sec- tor represents roughly 12% of total primary energy use in the BC economy.
10: The greenhouse gas emission reduction in the eco-industrial system is 1360 tonne CO2-equiv/year (Section 4.2.2). Scaling this impact reduction to the entire dairy sector in BC comes to a potential reduction of nearly 300kt CO2-equiv/year. The overall impact from the agriculture sector in BC was reported as around 1 Mt CO2-equiv in 2010 (Nyboer and Kniewasser, 2012). Therefore, the potential reduction accounts for 30% of all the climate change impacts from the agricultural sector.
11: There have been studies concerning the acidification and eutrophication risks due to manure and fertilizer applica- tions in BC but no thorough auditing of these risks has been reported. To normalize the impact results of this study, Cana- dian average normalization factors are used. Lautier et al. (2010) reported the factors as SO2-equiv/person/year and 15.8kg PO4-equiv/person/year for acidification and eutro- phication, respectively. Given the population of BC as about 4.6 million (Statistics Canada, 2012), the total acidification and eutrophication impacts in BC are 713 million kg SO2- equiv/year and 73 million kg PO4-equiv/year, respectively. Thus the potential reduction of acidification from BC's dairy industry can be calculated as around 0.25% of the total. In the same way the increase of eutrophication is about 0.064%. The results show that the changes of the acidification and eutrophication impacts are insignificant. In fact, based on the

13: Respiratory effects from inorganic emissions increase by 94kg PM2.5-equiv/year in the eco-industrial system compared to the base scenario. For the whole dairy industry, the increase would be around 20 tonne PM2.5-equiv/year. Again, the national nor- malization factor, of 53kg PM2.5-equiv/person/year, is used because no local data are available. Multiplied by BC's pop- ulation, the total for the province comes to 700 million kg PM2.5-equiv/year. Thus the increase in respiratory effect by conducting the eco-industrial integration is very small.

For the respiratory effect caused by organic emissions, no Canadian normalization factor is available. The factor devel- oped for Western Europe is therefore used as a tentative alternative, leading to an estimate for BC's population of 15 million tonne C2H4-equiv/year. The reduction from eco- industrial integration is 35 kg C2H4-equiv/year for the studied system and 7.4 tonne C2H4-equiv/year for the entire dairy sec- tor. This value is negligibly small compared to the total for BC.
14: Human toxicity impact is also analyzed using Canadian nor- malization factors reported by Lautier et al. (2010) for human carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic toxicity, respectively. An average value of 200 kg C2H3Cl-equiv/person/year is used in this study, leading to an estimate of up to 920 million kg/year as the total impact from BC. The potential year-round reduction in the entire dairy sector comes to 0.46 million kg C2H3Cl-equiv/year, corresponding to 0.05% of the total. This is also not significant.
15: The results of a Monte Carlo analysis with 100 runs are shown in Table 5.

The Monte Carlo results show that, even allowing for uncer- tainty, the difference between systems is significant in all impact categories so that the conclusions from the life cycle impact analysis seem to hold. For non-renewable energy con- sumption, climate change, acidification, respiratory organics, and human toxicity, the impact from base scenario is always (100%) higher than from the integrated system. For eutro- phication and respiratory inorganics, the impact from the integrated system has an 80% probability of being higher than in the base scenario. This confirms the small impact increase in the integrated system as stated in Section 4.2.
16: This study finds that the integrated dairy farm-greenhouse eco-industrial system reduces non-renewable energy con- sumption, climate change impact, aquatic acidification, respiratory effects from organic emissions and human toxic- ity, all by more than 40% compared to conventional operations even if the surplus digestate has to be composted as a waste. However, the impacts are slightly increased in categories of aquatic eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions. If the surplus digestate can be all utilized to replace sawdust and chemical fertilizers, the impacts are reduced in all categories including eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions, with percentage reductions range from about 20% to over 100%. Compared to the regional inven- tories, more than 10% of the total impacts in BC and up to 30% of the total impacts in BC's agriculture sector can be reduced in the moderate scenario; while the changes in the other impact categories are all less than 1% of the total inventories in BC. However, it should be noted that the inventory data used for normalization of these impact categories are estimated based on the national average or European average values because of the lack of BC specific data.

Although the results cannot be directly compared with published studies as each LCA study has its own spe- cific system boundaries and data sources with particular assumptions, the conclusions from this study are generally in agreement with the literature. Reductions of non-renewable energy consumption and climate change are reported by other studies (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Börjesson and Berglund, 2006, 2007; de Vries et al., 2010; Venczel and Powers, 2010; Mezzullo et al., 2012). Berglund and Börjesson (2006), Börjesson and Berglund (2006, 2007) and Lansche and Müller (2012) reported that AD also reduces acidification and eutro- phication impacts if the digestate is all utilized for substituting other materials, consistent with the results of the optimistic case in this study. de Vries et al. (2010) observed similar impacts in the base scenario for acidification and eutrophi- cation from dairy manure AD, while Mezzullo et al. (2012) found that the use of biogas and digestate increased acid- ification and eutrophication effects. Mezzullo et al. (2012) also reported higher human respiratory effects from the use of biogas and digestate, consistent with the current finding.

Overall, the eco-industrial integration of dairy farm and greenhouse shows substantial potential environmental ben- efits. BC has been enthusiastic in installing ADs, and there are already two digesters running at farms in the province. Digestion with multiple organic waste streams is also being developed, which could improve the performance of the integrated eco-industrial system. Also, to further evaluate
such an integrated system, comprehensive economic and pol- icy analyses should be conducted along with environmental assessments.

17: This study finds that the integrated dairy farm-greenhouse eco-industrial system reduces non-renewable energy con- sumption, climate change impact, aquatic acidification, respiratory effects from organic emissions and human toxic- ity, all by more than 40% compared to conventional operations even if the surplus digestate has to be composted as a waste. However, the impacts are slightly increased in categories of aquatic eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions. If the surplus digestate can be all utilized to replace sawdust and chemical fertilizers, the impacts are reduced in all categories including eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions, with percentage reductions range from about 20% to over 100%. Compared to the regional inven- tories, more than 10% of the total impacts in BC and up to 30% of the total impacts in BC's agriculture sector can be reduced in the moderate scenario; while the changes in the other impact categories are all less than 1% of the total inventories in BC. However, it should be noted that the inventory data used for normalization of these impact categories are estimated based on the national average or European average values because of the lack of BC specific data.

Although the results cannot be directly compared with published studies as each LCA study has its own spe- cific system boundaries and data sources with particular assumptions, the conclusions from this study are generally in agreement with the literature. Reductions of non-renewable energy consumption and climate change are reported by other studies (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Börjesson and Berglund, 2006, 2007; de Vries et al., 2010; Venczel and Powers, 2010; Mezzullo et al., 2012). Berglund and Börjesson (2006), Börjesson and Berglund (2006, 2007) and Lansche and Müller (2012) reported that AD also reduces acidification and eutro- phication impacts if the digestate is all utilized for substituting other materials, consistent with the results of the optimistic case in this study. de Vries et al. (2010) observed similar impacts in the base scenario for acidification and eutrophi- cation from dairy manure AD, while Mezzullo et al. (2012) found that the use of biogas and digestate increased acid- ification and eutrophication effects. Mezzullo et al. (2012) also reported higher human respiratory effects from the use of biogas and digestate, consistent with the current finding.

Overall, the eco-industrial integration of dairy farm and greenhouse shows substantial potential environmental ben- efits. BC has been enthusiastic in installing ADs, and there are already two digesters running at farms in the province. Digestion with multiple organic waste streams is also being developed, which could improve the performance of the integrated eco-industrial system. Also, to further evaluate
such an integrated system, comprehensive economic and pol- icy analyses should be conducted along with environmental assessments.

19: This study finds that the integrated dairy farm-greenhouse eco-industrial system reduces non-renewable energy con- sumption, climate change impact, aquatic acidification, respiratory effects from organic emissions and human toxic- ity, all by more than 40% compared to conventional operations even if the surplus digestate has to be composted as a waste. However, the impacts are slightly increased in categories of aquatic eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions. If the surplus digestate can be all utilized to replace sawdust and chemical fertilizers, the impacts are reduced in all categories including eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions, with percentage reductions range from about 20% to over 100%. Compared to the regional inven- tories, more than 10% of the total impacts in BC and up to 30% of the total impacts in BC's agriculture sector can be reduced in the moderate scenario; while the changes in the other impact categories are all less than 1% of the total inventories in BC. However, it should be noted that the inventory data used for normalization of these impact categories are estimated based on the national average or European average values because of the lack of BC specific data.

Although the results cannot be directly compared with published studies as each LCA study has its own spe- cific system boundaries and data sources with particular assumptions, the conclusions from this study are generally in agreement with the literature. Reductions of non-renewable energy consumption and climate change are reported by other studies (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Börjesson and Berglund, 2006, 2007; de Vries et al., 2010; Venczel and Powers, 2010; Mezzullo et al., 2012). Berglund and Börjesson (2006), Börjesson and Berglund (2006, 2007) and Lansche and Müller (2012) reported that AD also reduces acidification and eutro- phication impacts if the digestate is all utilized for substituting other materials, consistent with the results of the optimistic case in this study. de Vries et al. (2010) observed similar impacts in the base scenario for acidification and eutrophi- cation from dairy manure AD, while Mezzullo et al. (2012) found that the use of biogas and digestate increased acid- ification and eutrophication effects. Mezzullo et al. (2012) also reported higher human respiratory effects from the use of biogas and digestate, consistent with the current finding.

Overall, the eco-industrial integration of dairy farm and greenhouse shows substantial potential environmental ben- efits. BC has been enthusiastic in installing ADs, and there are already two digesters running at farms in the province. Digestion with multiple organic waste streams is also being developed, which could improve the performance of the integrated eco-industrial system. Also, to further evaluate
such an integrated system, comprehensive economic and pol- icy analyses should be conducted along with environmental assessments.

21: This study finds that the integrated dairy farm-greenhouse eco-industrial system reduces non-renewable energy con- sumption, climate change impact, aquatic acidification, respiratory effects from organic emissions and human toxic- ity, all by more than 40% compared to conventional operations even if the surplus digestate has to be composted as a waste. However, the impacts are slightly increased in categories of aquatic eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions. If the surplus digestate can be all utilized to replace sawdust and chemical fertilizers, the impacts are reduced in all categories including eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions, with percentage reductions range from about 20% to over 100%. Compared to the regional inven- tories, more than 10% of the total impacts in BC and up to 30% of the total impacts in BC's agriculture sector can be reduced in the moderate scenario; while the changes in the other impact categories are all less than 1% of the total inventories in BC. However, it should be noted that the inventory data used for normalization of these impact categories are estimated based on the national average or European average values because of the lack of BC specific data.

Although the results cannot be directly compared with published studies as each LCA study has its own spe- cific system boundaries and data sources with particular assumptions, the conclusions from this study are generally in agreement with the literature. Reductions of non-renewable energy consumption and climate change are reported by other studies (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Börjesson and Berglund, 2006, 2007; de Vries et al., 2010; Venczel and Powers, 2010; Mezzullo et al., 2012). Berglund and Börjesson (2006), Börjesson and Berglund (2006, 2007) and Lansche and Müller (2012) reported that AD also reduces acidification and eutro- phication impacts if the digestate is all utilized for substituting other materials, consistent with the results of the optimistic case in this study. de Vries et al. (2010) observed similar impacts in the base scenario for acidification and eutrophi- cation from dairy manure AD, while Mezzullo et al. (2012) found that the use of biogas and digestate increased acid- ification and eutrophication effects. Mezzullo et al. (2012) also reported higher human respiratory effects from the use of biogas and digestate, consistent with the current finding.

Overall, the eco-industrial integration of dairy farm and greenhouse shows substantial potential environmental ben- efits. BC has been enthusiastic in installing ADs, and there are already two digesters running at farms in the province. Digestion with multiple organic waste streams is also being developed, which could improve the performance of the integrated eco-industrial system. Also, to further evaluate
such an integrated system, comprehensive economic and pol- icy analyses should be conducted along with environmental assessments.

23: <header>5. Conclusions </header>

This study finds that the integrated dairy farm-greenhouse eco-industrial system reduces non-renewable energy con- sumption, climate change impact, aquatic acidification, respiratory effects from organic emissions and human toxic- ity, all by more than 40% compared to conventional operations even if the surplus digestate has to be composted as a waste. However, the impacts are slightly increased in categories of aquatic eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions. If the surplus digestate can be all utilized to replace sawdust and chemical fertilizers, the impacts are reduced in all categories including eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions, with percentage reductions range from about 20% to over 100%. Compared to the regional inven- tories, more than 10% of the total impacts in BC and up to 30% of the total impacts in BC's agriculture sector can be reduced in the moderate scenario; while the changes in the other impact categories are all less than 1% of the total inventories in BC. However, it should be noted that the inventory data used for normalization of these impact categories are estimated based on the national average or European average values because of the lack of BC specific data.

Although the results cannot be directly compared with published studies as each LCA study has its own spe- cific system boundaries and data sources with particular assumptions, the conclusions from this study are generally in agreement with the literature. Reductions of non-renewable energy consumption and climate change are reported by other studies (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Börjesson and Berglund, 2006, 2007; de Vries et al., 2010; Venczel and Powers, 2010; Mezzullo et al., 2012). Berglund and Börjesson (2006), Börjesson and Berglund (2006, 2007) and Lansche and Müller (2012) reported that AD also reduces acidification and eutro- phication impacts if the digestate is all utilized for substituting other materials, consistent with the results of the optimistic case in this study. de Vries et al. (2010) observed similar impacts in the base scenario for acidification and eutrophi- cation from dairy manure AD, while Mezzullo et al. (2012) found that the use of biogas and digestate increased acid- ification and eutrophication effects. Mezzullo et al. (2012) also reported higher human respiratory effects from the use of biogas and digestate, consistent with the current finding.

Overall, the eco-industrial integration of dairy farm and greenhouse shows substantial potential environmental ben- efits. BC has been enthusiastic in installing ADs, and there are already two digesters running at farms in the province. Digestion with multiple organic waste streams is also being developed, which could improve the performance of the integrated eco-industrial system. Also, to further evaluate
such an integrated system, comprehensive economic and pol- icy analyses should be conducted along with environmental assessments.

This study finds that the integrated dairy farm-greenhouse eco-industrial system reduces non-renewable energy con- sumption, climate change impact, aquatic acidification, respiratory effects from organic emissions and human toxic- ity, all by more than 40% compared to conventional operations even if the surplus digestate has to be composted as a waste. However, the impacts are slightly increased in categories of aquatic eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions. If the surplus digestate can be all utilized to replace sawdust and chemical fertilizers, the impacts are reduced in all categories including eutrophication and respiratory effects from inorganic emissions, with percentage reductions range from about 20% to over 100%. Compared to the regional inven- tories, more than 10% of the total impacts in BC and up to 30% of the total impacts in BC's agriculture sector can be reduced in the moderate scenario; while the changes in the other impact categories are all less than 1% of the total inventories in BC. However, it should be noted that the inventory data used for normalization of these impact categories are estimated based on the national average or European average values because of the lack of BC specific data.

Although the results cannot be directly compared with published studies as each LCA study has its own spe- cific system boundaries and data sources with particular assumptions, the conclusions from this study are generally in agreement with the literature. Reductions of non-renewable energy consumption and climate change are reported by other studies (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Börjesson and Berglund, 2006, 2007; de Vries et al., 2010; Venczel and Powers, 2010; Mezzullo et al., 2012). Berglund and Börjesson (2006), Börjesson and Berglund (2006, 2007) and Lansche and Müller (2012) reported that AD also reduces acidification and eutro- phication impacts if the digestate is all utilized for substituting other materials, consistent with the results of the optimistic case in this study. de Vries et al. (2010) observed similar impacts in the base scenario for acidification and eutrophi- cation from dairy manure AD, while Mezzullo et al. (2012) found that the use of biogas and digestate increased acid- ification and eutrophication effects. Mezzullo et al. (2012) also reported higher human respiratory effects from the use of biogas and digestate, consistent with the current finding.

Overall, the eco-industrial integration of dairy farm and greenhouse shows substantial potential environmental ben- efits. BC has been enthusiastic in installing ADs, and there are already two digesters running at farms in the province. Digestion with multiple organic waste streams is also being developed, which could improve the performance of the integrated eco-industrial system. Also, to further evaluate
such an integrated system, comprehensive economic and pol- icy analyses should be conducted along with environmental assessments.
